insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

The dependency graph flows cleanly from schema -> core -> store -> cli with no circular dependencies, indicating strong architectural discipline for a 36-file single-zone codebase.
The analyze module (scanners, propose, reconcile) is a well-isolated subsystem that could be extracted into its own zone if the project grows, since it has minimal coupling to core.
The MCP server in cli/mcp.ts at ~405 lines is the largest single file and serves as a second entry point alongside the CLI dispatcher, making it a natural seam for future zone splitting.

The store layer (file-adapter.ts) imports core/tree.js and core/canonical.js, creating a dependency inversion: store depends on core business logic instead of being a dumb persistence layer. Layering should be schema ← core ← store, but store reaches sideways into core.
PRIORITY_ORDER is duplicated three times (core/canonical.ts, core/next-task.ts, analyze/propose.ts) — a hidden coupling via copy-paste that risks drift if priority tiers change.
CLI commands init.ts and validate.ts bypass the store abstraction by directly using node:fs/promises for config file I/O, undermining the adapter pattern.
The core layer has no barrel export (no src/core/index.ts), so consumers import from individual files (core/tree.js, core/next-task.js), making the public API surface implicit rather than explicit.
REX_DIR constant is duplicated: declared in cli/commands/constants.ts and re-declared in cli/mcp.ts, creating a sync risk for the two entry points.

sortByPriority logic is duplicated between core/canonical.ts:14-27 (sortItems, recursive) and core/next-task.ts:11-18 (sortByPriority, shallow) — two subtly different sort implementations within the same core layer that can diverge.

MCP tool handlers in cli/mcp.ts reimplement the same orchestration logic as CLI commands (e.g., get_next_task duplicates cmdNext, update_task_status duplicates cmdUpdate) rather than sharing a common service layer, creating a parallel maintenance burden across ~400 lines.

The PRDItem interface in schema/v1.ts:21 has an index signature `[key: string]: unknown` alongside explicit fields, which defeats TypeScript's excess-property checking and allows arbitrary data to pass type checks silently — the `.passthrough()` on the Zod schema compounds this by accepting unknown keys at runtime too.

readLog() at store/file-adapter.ts:105 casts each parsed JSON line as LogEntry without validation (`JSON.parse(line) as LogEntry`), while appendLog() validates via Zod before writing — asymmetric serialization boundary means corrupted log files can inject malformed entries into the system.

The analyze --accept flow in cli/commands/analyze.ts:125-169 calls store.addItem() sequentially in a loop (one file write per item) with no batching or transaction, so a failure mid-loop leaves the PRD in a partially-updated state with no rollback.

update.ts:6-12 hardcodes VALID_STATUSES and VALID_PRIORITIES as string Sets instead of deriving them from the Zod enums in schema/validate.ts (ItemStatusSchema, PrioritySchema), creating a second source of truth that will drift if enum values change.
callSourceVisionMCP in recommend.ts:33-78 uses raw child_process.spawn with callback-based stream handling, but never sets a timeout — a hung sourcevision process will block the CLI indefinitely.

The PRDItemSchema in validate.ts:18 is typed as `z.ZodType<Record<string, unknown>>` erasing the inferred Zod type, so validateDocument returns `Record<string, unknown>` forcing every callsite to cast `as PRDDocument` — the Zod schema is well-typed internally but the export discards the type information.

PRIORITY_ORDER is defined three times (canonical.ts:7, next-task.ts:4, propose.ts:25) with two different type signatures — propose.ts uses Record<string, number> (loose) while the other two use Record<Priority, number> (strict), creating a silent type-safety gap
The adapter field in RexConfig is dead configuration — all 8 createStore() call sites hardcode the literal string "file" instead of reading config.adapter, making alternative adapters unreachable

Log event names for the same semantic operation differ between CLI and MCP: update.ts:68 emits "item_updated" while mcp.ts:129 emits "status_changed" for status changes, with no shared event-name constants
init.ts and validate.ts both bypass the store abstraction layer, using raw fs readFile/writeFile directly, making them incompatible with any non-file adapter

Zone cohesion and coupling scores for any zone containing an HTML entry point should be treated as lower bounds, not accurate measures.

prd-tree-lifecycle-tests and prd-tree-node-culler are merge candidates strengthened by import data: both zones contain only test-only-imported source files, occupy the same source directory, and exhibit identical structural form. Merging them into a single 'prd-tree-utilities' zone reduces zone count by one without sacrificing isolation, and co-locating all three source files makes a future dead-code determination easier to act on as a single decision.

The four messaging utilities form a pipeline whose correct composition order is documented only in an integration test helper, not in production code. This inverts the normal relationship between code and tests: the test file is the specification, and production code is the loose collection of parts — a structural reversal that will degrade over time as the two evolve independently.

The messaging directory (packages/web/src/viewer/messaging/) has four source files sharing a uniform factory-pattern API but no index.ts. Every consumer must import four separate modules by name. A single barrel export would make the messaging subsystem a first-class module boundary, eliminating the zone fragmentation, reducing web-viewer's import surface from 7 to 1, and surfacing the pipeline as an intentional design rather than an emergent coupling pattern.

No automated import boundary enforcement exists (no ESLint import-no-restricted-paths rules, no dependency-cruiser configuration, no CI gate). The four-tier dependency hierarchy and gateway pattern are enforced entirely by convention — a developer adding a direct cross-tier import in any leaf file will bypass the architecture with no automated rejection and the violation will only surface during code review if a reviewer recognizes it.

All workspace package dependencies resolve as 'external' in the static import graph, causing zone coupling scores to structurally underreport real cross-package dependency depth for every zone. The coupling metric is blind to intended workspace consumption, making it impossible to use sourcevision's coupling scores to reason about compliance with the four-tier dependency hierarchy — a separate cross-package dependency audit tool or lint rule is required for that purpose.

The absence of a shared types package forces independent type definition across domain packages. Types that conceptually span rex and hench (task IDs, status enums, tree node shapes) are defined in rex and consumed by hench via gateway re-exports — if rex ever restructures its type definitions, hench and web must both update their gateway imports simultaneously. A dedicated @n-dx/types foundation package would decouple type evolution from domain logic changes.

Three production files across two zones (lazy-children.ts, listener-lifecycle.ts, node-culler.ts) have no production importers anywhere in the codebase — confirmed by full import graph traversal. If dynamic import() is not in use, these files are dead code kept alive only by unit tests. A build-output audit (checking whether these modules appear in any compiled bundle) would definitively resolve the ambiguity and either confirm dead-code removal candidates or document the dynamic import contract.

The web package has no enforced boundary between its Node.js server layer (src/server/) and its browser client layer (src/viewer/). Both are in the same zone with the same import rules. A tsconfig path alias, package.json exports condition, or lint rule scoped to each subdirectory would make cross-environment imports a compile-time or lint error rather than a silent runtime failure.

The canonical messaging pipeline wiring (throttle → coalescer → rateLimiter → dedup) exists only in test infrastructure. Any production consumer that re-implements this wiring (e.g. a second viewer or a server-side handler) must rediscover the correct composition order from the test file. Extracting a createMessagingPipeline factory to production code would make the pipeline order explicit, testable as a unit, and prevent divergence.

The messaging directory lacks an index.ts despite containing four cohesive utilities with uniform API shapes. The absence of a barrel export is the root cause of the three-zone fragmentation, the 7 cross-zone import edges, and the below-threshold cohesion scores in viewer-message-flow-control and viewer-request-deduplication. Adding messaging/index.ts is the minimum viable fix that resolves all three symptoms simultaneously.

DEFAULT_CODEX_MODEL = "gpt-5-codex" is duplicated across three domain packages (rex, hench, sourcevision) with no shared definition, because @n-dx/llm-client defines it as a private unexported constant. The foundation layer holds the authoritative value but does not expose it — making the architectural intent (domain packages depend on the foundation layer) unenforceable for this specific constant. Exporting model-name defaults from llm-client's public.ts would close this gap without changing any dependency relationships.

The viewer layer has three co-located subdirectories (messaging/, polling/, performance/) that all contain cohesive, factory-pattern utilities with multiple consumers and no barrel export. The pattern is structural: a viewer convention of keeping subdirectories as flat file collections rather than bounded modules. A single team decision to add index.ts to each of these three directories would resolve the zone-fragmentation, inconsistent import depths, and consumer-side wiring duplication documented across multiple existing insights — the fix is mechanical once the convention is agreed upon.

The zone previously identified in analysis passes as 'web-integration' is the current zone 'viewer-call-rate-limiter' (call-rate-limiter.ts, its unit test, and the co-located request-dedup.test.ts). The single production import into this zone from the viewer hub (use-prd-data.ts → call-rate-limiter.ts) is a production-to-production import within the messaging subsystem — not a layer violation. Multiple earlier-pass insights that characterized this as 'production importing from integration-test scope' were based on a zone-name misread. The zone's below-threshold cohesion (0.67) is caused by request-dedup.test.ts being Louvain-clustered with call-rate-limiter.ts due to import proximity, not by an architectural boundary violation.

The zone previously identified as 'web-4' (3 files, cohesion 0.75) is the current zone 'dom-performance-monitoring' (dom-performance-monitor.ts, use-dom-performance-monitor.ts, and its test). use-dom-performance-monitor.ts has zero confirmed static importers anywhere in the codebase, making it a fifth production file with no static consumer — alongside lazy-children.ts, listener-lifecycle.ts, node-culler.ts, and now the hook variant. The 'anomalous import direction' concern flagged in earlier passes (web-4 importing into the hub) does not reflect an architectural inversion; a performance monitoring hook naturally depends on viewer infrastructure. The concern was an artifact of the opaque zone name 'web-4' masking semantic intent.

Earlier enrichment passes accumulated incorrect architectural conclusions because analysis was conducted against opaque positional zone IDs (web-4, web-5, web-6, web-integration) rather than semantic names. The 'layer violation' finding (production → test zone import), the 'anomalous import direction' finding (web-4 into hub), and the 'web-integration as integration-test scope' interpretation are all misdiagnoses traceable to zone-name opacity. This is a systemic risk: enrichment passes are cumulative, and a false conclusion added in pass N propagates to all subsequent passes as an unchallenged prior. Semantic zone naming must precede multi-pass architectural analysis, and enrichment results should be re-reviewed whenever zone IDs are renamed.

Five confirmed production files across three zones (dom-performance-monitoring, prd-tree-lifecycle-tests, prd-tree-node-culler) have zero static importers. All five are viewer-layer files, all five are in the src/viewer/ subtree, and none are in the src/server/ subtree. The zero-importer pattern is entirely concentrated in the viewer layer — the server layer has no equivalent orphaned files. This concentration suggests a viewer-specific development pattern (possibly files written for planned features or extracted from a larger component before integration was completed) rather than a codebase-wide dead-code problem.

No CODEOWNERS file exists in the repository root. As zone boundaries, gateway modules, and four-tier dependency rules are the sole architectural enforcement mechanisms, assigning file-level ownership via CODEOWNERS would provide a second enforcement layer: automated reviewer assignment for gateway files, cross-tier imports, and zone boundary files would catch violations at PR time even when the reviewer pool is unfamiliar with the full architecture.

All four messaging source files (message-coalescer.ts, message-throttle.ts, call-rate-limiter.ts, request-dedup.ts) share the same physical directory (packages/web/src/viewer/messaging/) but are distributed across three separate zones — a directory-to-zone mismatch structurally unique in the codebase. In every other package, files in the same source directory occupy the same zone. The fragmentation is caused by cross-cutting imports within the directory rather than external coupling, which is itself evidence the directory lacks an enforced internal API boundary.

Four of the five analyzed zones originate from packages/web/src/viewer/ but only three use the 'viewer-' zone prefix. A naming convention tying zone prefix to source directory path (all zones rooted in src/viewer/ prefixed with 'viewer-') would make zone membership predictable from file location alone and eliminate the current inconsistency.

The four messaging utilities implement a uniform factory pattern (createX / Config / Runtime) but this shared contract is undocumented and unenforced. If a future utility is added to the messaging directory without following the pattern, a barrel index.ts will silently expose an inconsistent API. Declaring a MessagingUtility<TConfig, TRuntime> generic interface would make the contract explicit and give TypeScript a way to reject non-conforming additions at compile time.

@n-dx/llm-client's public.ts does not export DEFAULT_CODEX_MODEL or DEFAULT_MODEL, forcing three domain packages to independently hardcode the same string literals. Add these constants to llm-client's public API so the foundation layer is the single source of truth for default model names, consistent with the stated four-tier dependency contract where domain packages depend on foundation rather than duplicating its values.

Three viewer subdirectories (messaging/, polling/, performance/) all lack index.ts barrel exports despite containing cohesive utilities consumed by 4, 11, and 12 files respectively. Adopting a monorepo-wide convention that every utility subdirectory with 3+ consumers exposes an index.ts would retroactively fix all three directories and prevent the pattern from recurring in new subdirectories.

Findings that characterize the web-viewer → web-integration import as a layer violation should be removed or reclassified as correct production-to-production imports.

Run a Vite bundle output audit targeting these five modules specifically to confirm whether they appear in any compiled chunk. Record the result as a comment in each file — either documenting the dynamic import() call site or marking the file as a dead-code removal candidate.

Adopting a convention where zone names derive from their source directory path (src/viewer/* → viewer-*) would make the zone map self-documenting and eliminate the current outlier.

Introducing a shared MessagingUtility<TConfig, TRuntime> generic interface would formalize the contract, enable TypeScript to catch non-conforming future additions, and document the intended pattern for the messaging directory.

Zone naming inconsistency across analysis passes is a methodology defect, not a code defect. Findings 33 and 34 confirm that opaque aliases used in passes 1–3 (web-integration, web-5, web-6) did not map to the semantic zone IDs now in zones.json. Findings 14 and 16 both derive their conclusions from the incorrect identification of viewer-call-rate-limiter as an integration-test zone. Both findings are misdiagnoses and have been downgraded. Any remediation effort should verify zone identity against the current zones.json before acting on pass-1–3 findings.

All 5 circular dependency cycles share an identical structural cause: analysis pipeline stages with interdependent type contracts. The pattern appears in rex/src/analyze/ (reason.ts ↔ extract.ts ↔ file-validation.ts) and sourcevision/src/analyzers/ (enrich.ts ↔ zones.ts). Both subsystems would benefit from the same architectural solution — a thin type-only module each pair can import from rather than from each other.

A dead-code cluster exists exclusively within src/viewer/: findings 28, 31, 40, 41, and 46 all identify zero-importer exports or production files with no importers in this subdirectory (lazy-children.ts, listener-lifecycle.ts, node-culler.ts, use-dom-performance-monitor.ts, loader.ts, polling-restart.ts). Concentration in one subdirectory suggests an incomplete or abandoned refactoring effort rather than scattered organic dead code. A Vite bundle audit targeting these six files specifically is the fastest way to resolve the ambiguity.

The four-tier dependency hierarchy is architecturally sound by convention but operationally unverifiable. Findings 22 and 23 together show that workspace imports are invisible to the coupling metric AND no tooling gates exist. The hierarchy cannot be monitored or enforced with current infrastructure. Both the metric blind spot and the absence of lint rules must be addressed together for the four-tier contract to have operational meaning.

The missing barrel exports (messaging, polling, performance) and the absence of import boundary enforcement are the two systemic root causes behind the majority of warnings in this analysis. Fixing messaging/index.ts resolves 6 findings. Adding dependency-cruiser resolves 2 findings and provides ongoing protection. All other findings are either downstream symptoms of these two issues or standalone point defects.

The gateway pattern successfully prevents all inter-package circular dependencies — findings 10 and 11 both confirm zero cross-package cycles exist. The 5 cycles are strictly intra-package. This confirms that the gateway pattern is working as intended at the macro level, and the remaining cycle hygiene work is a local concern within two subsystems rather than a systemic architectural risk.

CONTRADICTION RESOLUTION: Global findings 14 and 16 are misdiagnoses caused by zone naming inconsistency. Finding 16 characterizes the web-viewer → web-integration import as a 'test-layer isolation violation'; finding 14 characterizes web-integration as a 'partially-realized abstraction layer'. Both conclusions depend on web-integration being an integration-test zone. Finding 33 establishes it is viewer-call-rate-limiter, a production messaging utility. The import is a valid production-to-production dependency. Both findings have been downgraded to info. No remediation is warranted.

Introduce packages/rex/src/analyze/analysis-types.ts containing the shared interfaces currently causing the reason.ts ↔ extract.ts and reason.ts ↔ extract.ts ↔ file-validation.ts cycles. Both analysis modules import from their respective types module rather than from each other. Two files break all 5 circular dependencies using the same pattern, which is consistent with the shared structural cause identified across findings 10, 11, 12, 18, and 19.

Add a dependency-cruiser configuration at the monorepo root codifying the four-tier hierarchy as enforced rules: Orchestration may import Execution; Execution may import Domain; Domain packages may not import each other; Foundation may not import above its tier. This single config addition converts finding 23 (convention-only enforcement) from a standing risk into a CI gate, and addresses finding 22 (workspace deps invisible to coupling metrics) simultaneously.

Export DEFAULT_MODEL and DEFAULT_CODEX_MODEL from packages/llm-client/src/public.ts and remove the independently hardcoded string literals in the three domain packages that duplicate them. This is a pure constant-centralization change with no logic impact. The foundation layer becomes the authoritative source for model name strings, making finding 30 a closed issue and strengthening the four-tier contract at a concrete file level.
